{{Short description|Physical laws describing the relationship between a body and forces}}
{{Infobox physics
| name = Newton's laws of motion
| image = Newtons laws in latin.jpg
| caption = Newton's First and Second Laws, in Latin, from the original 1687 ''Principia''
| topics = [[Inertia]], [[Force]], [[Momentum]], [[Action and reaction]]
}}

'''Newton's laws of motion''' are three physical laws that, together, laid the foundation for [[classical mechanics]]. They describe the relationship between a body and the [[force]]s acting upon it, and the body's [[motion]] in response to those forces. First published by [[Isaac Newton]] in his ''[[Philosophiæ Naturalis Principia Mathematica]]'' (1687), these laws have been expressed in many forms over the centuries.

== First law (Law of inertia) ==
{{Main|Inertia}}
:''Every object perseveres in its state of rest, or of uniform motion in a right line, unless it is compelled to change that state by forces impressed thereon.'' (Newton's original Latin)

In modern terms:
:<math>\sum \mathbf{F} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{d\mathbf{v}}{dt} = 0</math>

A body remains at rest or in uniform motion (constant velocity) unless acted upon by a net external force. This law defines [[inertial reference frame]]s: frames in which the first law holds.

== Second law ==
The net force on a body equals the rate of change of its [[momentum]]:
:<math>\mathbf{F} = \frac{d\mathbf{p}}{dt}</math>

For constant mass:
:<math>\mathbf{F} = m\mathbf{a} = m\frac{d\mathbf{v}}{dt} = m\frac{d^2\mathbf{r}}{dt^2}</math>

This is the quantitative statement linking force to acceleration. The SI unit of force, the [[newton (unit)|newton]], is defined from this law.

Key properties:
* Force and acceleration are in the same direction
* The relationship is [[linear]] (<math>F \propto a</math>)
* The law applies to the [[net force]] (vector sum of all forces)

== Third law (Action and reaction) ==
:''To every action there is always opposed an equal reaction: or the mutual actions of two bodies upon each other are always equal, and directed to contrary parts.''

:<math>\mathbf{F}_{A \to B} = -\mathbf{F}_{B \to A}</math>

If body A exerts a force on body B, then body B simultaneously exerts a force on body A of equal magnitude and opposite direction.

Important implications:
* Forces always come in pairs
* The action and reaction forces act on '''different bodies''' — thus they do not cancel
* Applies to all types of forces (gravitational, electromagnetic, contact)

== Applications ==

=== Block on an inclined plane ===
For a block of mass <math>m</math> on a frictionless incline at angle <math>\theta</math>:
* Normal force: <math>N = mg\cos\theta</math>
* Acceleration down the incline: <math>a = g\sin\theta</math>

=== Atwood machine ===
{{Main|Atwood machine}}
For two masses connected by a massless string over a frictionless pulley:
:<math>a = \frac{m_2 - m_1}{m_1 + m_2}g</math>
:<math>T = \frac{2m_1 m_2}{m_1 + m_2}g</math>

=== Uniform circular motion ===
For an object moving at speed <math>v</math> in a circle of radius <math>r</math>:
:<math>a_c = \frac{v^2}{r}, \quad F_c = \frac{mv^2}{r}</math>

== Limitations ==
Newton's laws are excellent approximations for macroscopic objects at non-relativistic speeds. They break down:
* At speeds near <math>c</math> — replaced by [[special relativity]]
* At atomic scales — replaced by [[quantum mechanics]]
* In strong gravitational fields — replaced by [[general relativity]]

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Classical mechanics]]
* [[Momentum]]
* [[Force]]
* [[Inertia]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Newton's laws of motion| ]]
[[Category:Classical mechanics]]
[[Category:Isaac Newton]]
